user_prompt: ### Task: Playback
Help me implement `.wav` file playback

I have created `lib\WavPlayback\PlaybackView.cpp` and `lib\WavPlayback\WavPlayback.cpp` (and associated header files) to work with. `PlaybackView` should handle the GUI code as much as possible, while `WavPlayback` handles the reading of the file and the feeding of the audio into the speaker. Use `AudioManager` as much as possible. `PlaybackView` inherits from `FlyGuiView`
Clicking on a recorded file from the `ScrollView` will enter this view and start playback.
The duration of the file is an estimate based on file size, assuming 16 bit stereo PCM, and subtract the size of the RIFF header.
When playback is finished, go into pause mode (remember to change the icon).
### Scrub Bar

use `lib\WavPlayback\ScrubBar.cpp` and the associated header file to implement a scrubbing bar much like the `ProgressBar` in the sense that we can define a size and position of it. It also needs initialization parameters for total time. It needs setters for current position. It needs a callback that is inside `PlaybackView`  to handle touch events on the bar so that the `PlaybackView`  can delegate the actual file seek to `WavPlayback` and then call the update function of `ScrubBar`.
At the right side of screen, just above the `ScrubBar` itself, show the total file duration as “hh:mm:ss”

At the left side of the screen, just below the `ScrubBar` itself, show the current position in the file as “hh:mm:ss”, but in yellow text, and the same slightly bigger font used for the elapsed time during recording. Even if the code does something weird, do not let the displayed current time number exceed the estimated duration.
The bar itself is always blue, and since it is now capable of going backwards, the drawing of the bar needs to also draw a filled black rectangle after the filled blue rectangle
### The layout of the view
`sprite_record_50` in the top left corner, below the top bar. Beside this icon, show the file name, with a normal font, center aligned horizontally with the 50x50 icon
(note: I know we can fit 3 lines of text here)
The region from just below the icon, which I think is Y = 60, this is where we put a 300 pixel wide 12 pixel tall `ScrubBar` , horizontally centered

At the bottom of the screen will be three buttons, each should be centered as if the screen was divided in 3 columns and then the buttons centered in the columns. The icons are also tied to `BtnA` `BtnB` and `BtnC`
The left button is a play/pause toggle control. Use `sprite_playback_play_50` and `sprite_playback_pause_50` as appropriate

The center button is volume control, which cycles through 4 settings, use `sprite_speaker_50` for full volume, `sprite_speaker_66_50` for 66% volume, `sprite_speaker_33_50` for 33% volume, and `sprite_speaker_00_50` for 0% volume.
The right button is one that stops playback and quits, use `sprite_squarex_50`
### `WavPlayback`
This needs to open the file, get its size (based on file size, not RIFF data), estimate the duration.
Every pump action is 0.5 second worth of data. Pump the data into the `g_fifo_bt2spk`, we are borrowing it. Enable the speaker through `AudioManager` and we should get sound.
There are setters for playback position, which simply sets the file seek pointer intelligently.
A setter is needed for volume, which should use the volume function already implemented by `AudioManager`, I am pretty sure the application of the gain is already implemented

A setter is needed for the play/pause state, pausing means no more reading file content and enqueuing and clearing of the FIFO. The file seek pointer needs to be re-wound a bit according to how much of the FIFO was cleared.

The pump function I will call from almost the same place that `AudioFileRecorder::pump();` happens in `main.cpp`, because this module is like the opposite of `AudioFileRecorder` and I want it on the same thread/core for the same reasons (SPI sharing for LCD screen and microSD card)

`WavPlayback` is started when `PlaybackView` is loaded, and stopped (file handle closed) when unloaded. I am pretty sure by doing it this way will mean that an incoming bluetooth connection will automatically stop the playback, close the file, and start a recording, which is great.
### Future Steps
At this point, you can probably hand me something that is immediately testable. I already have a `.wav` file on the card.
I will tweak the UI, ask for bug fixes, and then see if I need to add more precise rewind and fast forwarding buttons.
agent_reply: - Added `WavPlayback` engine in [WavPlayback.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/WavPlayback.cpp): opens WAV, estimates duration from file size minus 44-byte RIFF header, seeks, pauses with FIFO rewind, sets volume, and pumps audio into `AudioManager::bluetoothToSpeakerFifo()`.
- Added `PlaybackView` in [PlaybackView.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/PlaybackView.cpp): file title, scrub bar, play/pause, 4-step volume, stop/exit.
- Wired `.wav` clicks from `ScrollView` into playback using the selected filename.
I also made playback mix stereo to mono before feeding the speaker FIFO, so overlapping mic/BT channels won’t silently drop the right channel.
Verification: `pio run -e thefly_sec0` passes. One intentional simplification: WAV parsing assumes the generated files are 16 kHz, stereo, 16-bit PCM with a 44-byte header, exactly as requested.

user_prompt: the positioning of the bottom row of buttons is all wrong
the left side's play/pause button is visible but clipped
the center has no visible icon
the right has no visible icon, but at some point I did see it overlayed on top of the play button
the scrub bar seems to start off screen and is not 300 pixels wide, it seems to terminate at the center of the screen
it smells like you used uint8_t instead of uint16_t for positioning?
playback works, the bar moves, the numbers change, so good progress
let me know if instructions that are too long are bad, that was a 2 pager if printed on paper
agent_reply: Fixed. The issue was exactly in that family, but not `uint8_t`: `PlaybackView` was doing `thefly_display.width()` during static construction, before `M5.begin()` initialized the display. That baked bad coordinates into the scrub bar and bottom buttons.
I changed the view so those positions are calculated in `layoutItems()` during `onLoad()`/redraw instead. Build passes with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`.

And no, long instructions are not bad. That two-pager was useful. The only catch is that long specs can hide lifecycle assumptions like “display dimensions are valid now,” so concrete test observations like this are gold.

user_prompt: weird, the speaker icon is responsive to touch, the quit button is responsive to touch, but not the play/pause button, it responds to the assigned button but not the screen touch
agent_reply: I made `PlaybackView::handleTouch()` explicitly route the entire bottom row by thirds:
It rewrites the touch event to the center of the corresponding icon before handing it to the button, so the left control no longer depends on the exact icon rectangle/hitbox.

user_prompt: while the time shown is in seconds, what actual unit is tracking time in this playback module?
agent_reply: The authoritative playback position is tracked in **bytes**, not seconds: `g_position_bytes` and `g_data_bytes` in [WavPlayback.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/WavPlayback.cpp:37).
The public/UI-facing unit is **milliseconds**. `durationMs()` and `positionMs()` convert byte position to ms with:
Then `PlaybackView` converts milliseconds to displayed seconds with `current / 1000` in [PlaybackView.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/PlaybackView.cpp:333).
`file byte offset` -> `milliseconds for scrub/UI state` -> `seconds only for display formatting`

user_prompt: `ScrubBar` needs a major tweak for what I want to do next
it needs customizable colours, the first one we have now stays blue. the color needs to be customizable at any time
a vertical hitbox start-y and end-y size needs to be defined explicitly relative to the given Y position. For the current bar already existing, it is from y minus 10 to y plus 30
whether or not it shows text is optional, for the one already here, it is true
the start and end time the bar represents need to be set-able by the owner, the current one has a start time of 0, the end time is already implemented but now expect it to change frequently
it needs to always exist, but whether it is hidden or not, and touchable or not, are both adjustable at any time.
I want to implement a more precise scrub bar. But only for long files that need it. It is yellow to hint that it is tied to the current time position.
This bar only shows up when paused, and only if the file is over 120 seconds long.
When the user pauses via the button or end-of-file, show the bar and enable the bar if the file is over 120 seconds long.

When shown, the bar is representing a start time that is the current time minus 30 seconds as the start time, and the end time is the current time plus 30 seconds (the whole bar represents 60 seconds always). The range does not go below 0 or above the actual file's duration. During a typical pause of a long file, this probably means the filled portion of the bar is right up to the middle.

When touched, figure out where to set the new time. Update the yellow time ticker text and the blue `SrubBar`. The yellow bar should also update it's represented current time but not the start or end time yet. The bar needs to look like it has reacted to the user's touch.

With every frame draw, make the start time and end time catch up to the current time position by 100ms worth (make this value easy to tweak). But respect the boundaries of the file. This will make the bar look like it is moving back towards the center, while the actual time position isn't actually changing.
The vertical hitbox for this new yellow bar is its Y to Y plus 40
If the user hits play, then hide the bar and disable it.
agent_reply: Implemented the `ScrubBar` upgrade and the long-file precision scrubber.
- `ScrubBar` now supports dynamic colors, explicit Y hitbox offsets, optional built-in time text, absolute start/end time ranges, and runtime visible/touchable control.
- Existing blue scrub bar now uses range `0..duration`, stays blue, shows the current/total time text, and has hitbox `y - 10` to `y + 30`.
- Added a second yellow precision scrub bar in `PlaybackView`.
- Yellow bar appears only when playback is paused or finished and the file duration is over 120 seconds.
- Yellow bar represents a 60-second window around current time, clamps at file boundaries, and moves its represented range back toward center by `kPrecisionCatchupMs = 100`.
- Touching yellow seeks immediately, updates the yellow ticker text through the blue bar, updates both bars’ positions, and leaves the yellow range in place so it visibly reacts before drifting back.
Build passed. I also restored `src/version.c` after PlatformIO touched it.
Implemented the `ScrubBar` upgrade and the long-file precision scrubber.
- `ScrubBar` now supports dynamic colors, explicit Y hitbox offsets, optional built-in time text, absolute start/end time ranges, and runtime visible/touchable control.

- Added a second yellow precision scrub bar in `PlaybackView`.
- Yellow bar appears only when playback is paused or finished and the file duration is over 120 seconds.
- Yellow bar represents a 60-second window around current time, clamps at file boundaries, and moves its represented range back toward center by `kPrecisionCatchupMs = 100`.
- Touching yellow seeks immediately, updates the yellow ticker text through the blue bar, updates both bars’ positions, and leaves the yellow range in place so it visibly reacts before drifting back.
Build passed. I also restored `src/version.c` after PlatformIO touched it.

user_prompt: `void PlaybackView::drawFileName() const` shall try to test a bigger font, see if it will exceed the screen, and use the current one if it does exceed
agent_reply: Done. `PlaybackView::drawFileName()` now tries `kLargeFileFont = 4` first, using a dry-run wrapper fit check against the available filename box. If it would overflow, it falls back to the existing `kNormalFont = 2` and current line height.

user_prompt: For context about our previous work on `PlaybackView`, see `.codex_history\lib\WavPlayback\PlaybackView.cpp.codexhist.json`

We will add a way to delete audio files, but not through the same mechanism that bluetooth unpairing is done. Instead, it will be done within the PlaybackView itself, because the time-stamp file names are not really good content identifiers, so I want the users to listen to the audio before deciding to delete.

When the audio is paused (both user pausing or end-of-file pausing), the delete button will be presented. It is first presented in a faded (darkened and dithered) state as `sprite_trash_50`, and if the user presses on it, then it transforms into a fully visible `sprite_trashconfirm_50`, if the user presses it again, then the file is deleted (do required destructors here), a `ModalDialog` confirmation is shown, and when dismissed, the `ScrollView` for files is shown.
Enforce a minimum debounce time of 1 second between the “arm” press and the “confirm” press.

The delete button will be in the top right corner of the screen, which is beside the file name. The file name right now has two possible fonts, which is decided based on the space available without this button. Do not change this calculation. When the delete button is shown, do not change the font, instead, calculate how many characters fit without touching the delete button, and only display those characters.
If the audio is scrubbed while paused, then the delete button returns to the faded un-armed state.
If the audio is played, the delete button disappears, the file name is redrawn in full, and becomes un-armed.
agent_reply: Implemented the delete flow in [PlaybackView.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/PlaybackView.cpp:521) and [PlaybackView.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/PlaybackView.h:31).

Paused playback now shows a top-right faded `sprite_trash_50`; first press arms it as `sprite_trashconfirm_50`; second press after at least 1 second stops playback, closes the WAV handle, deletes the SD file, refreshes the file `ScrollView`, and shows a `ModalDialog` before returning to the file list. Scrubbing while paused disarms it, and playing hides it and redraws the filename full-width again.
I kept the filename font decision using the original available width, then clipped only the displayed characters when the delete button is visible.
Build passed. I left the existing uncommitted sprite/version/doc changes alone; my code edits are limited to `PlaybackView`.

agent_reply: Done. When the delete button is armed, PlaybackView now draws `confirm delete?` in font 2 just left of the delete button, at the old just-above-blue-bar Y position.
I also made arming/disarming mark the frame dirty so the prompt appears and clears cleanly.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`; build passed.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: src/main.cpp
## My request for Codex:

We already have `WavPlayback`, and we have it in a namespace, turn it into a class object, with a base class `FilePlayback` and then `WavPlayback` will inherit from it. Also add `WavEncryptedPlayback` so that there is less branching on if statements.
Then implement `Mp3Playback` as another class that inherits `FilePlayback` , and in the same spirit, `Mp3EncryptedPlayback` as well

Whatever was using `WavPlayback` before will now simply use `FilePlayback` functions but decide if it constructs a `WavPlayback` or `Mp3Playback` or `WavEncryptedPlayback` or `Mp3EncryptedPlayback` based on file extension
Each one of these classes gets their own CPP and header file.

`Mp3EncryptedPlayback` and `WavEncryptedPlayback` should be inheriting from `Mp3Playback` and `WavPlayback` , and only offering decryption functions in between file reading and FIFO queuing, the file reading stuff and FIFO queueing should be inherited.
We retain the ability to playback `wav` files and `rec` files even if the MP3 compression is the recording method.

`PlaybackView` should not have to worry about file types, and handle seeking/scrubbing functions only based on time units. The playback class objects takes care of internally converting time units to file pointers.

For encrypted files, the time duration estimation only works with our defined sizes/lengths inside `inc\conf.h`, do not attempt to calculate file time duration by reading the actual file header, assume the encryption can only happen on files we record ourselves
For unencrypted files, the time duration estimation can come from just the header of the first MP3 frame. Assume it is constant bit rate.
I have placed `lib\libhelix-esp32-arduino` in the project, use `MP3DecoderHelix` for decoding as appropriate.
agent_reply: - Moved `PlaybackView` to construct playback by extension and talk only in time units through `FilePlayback`.
- Added Helix MP3 decode support, including a final sync probe so the last MP3 frame is flushed.
- Added `.mp3` / `.fly` to recent-file listing and disk stats while retaining `.wav` / `.rec`.
- Updated `src/main.cpp` to pump/stop through `PlaybackView` instead of static `WavPlayback` calls.
- `rg` found no remaining old `WavPlayback::` / namespace playback calls.
- `git diff --check` passes, aside from the repo’s CRLF warnings.

One note: `src/version.c` is dirty from the PlatformIO version generation step; I left it as-is. The build also still reports the existing ESP-FTP-Server `NetworkServer::available()` deprecation warning.
